title: Category-measurement discipline candidate
notes: Candidate generated from source-backed category-pressure cards. It tests whether category analysis, constructional function, distributional evidence, acceptability, licensing, and grammaticality attribution can be kept apart. It is not a general grammaticality account and is not scored.

edges.rationale: Distributional facts provide warrant for an analyst category or function assignment without making that assignment the grammatical target.
Construction-specific function can support a category analysis, but the evidential force depends on the diagnostic and theory being used.
Category-classification tasks can elicit analyst-style assignments that ordinary acceptability tasks do not.
Task wording can cue respondents to classify a category or function rather than report acceptability.
In category-pressure cases, fitting the constructional role can be part of licensing without settling the lexical-category analysis.
Analogical support can provide warrant for licensing in a constructional neighborhood.
Recoverable meaning can raise reported acceptability independently of category analysis.
Acceptability reports can measure licensing only through task-biased judgment channels.
Task framing can separate category classification, naturalness, correctness, and acceptability outputs.
Correctness ideology can shape explicit attribution without settling category analysis or community licensing.
Analyst category assignments can influence grammaticality attribution in metalinguistic contexts, but are not identical to attribution.
Reported acceptability provides noisy evidence for grammaticality attribution, especially outside category-classification tasks.
